title: Gall Bladder Stones
oneLiner: Gall stones form when bile in the gall bladder becomes too concentrated with cholesterol or bile salts, crystallising into stones.

cards.title: What is the Gall Bladder?
cards.body: The gall bladder is a small pouch under the liver. It stores bile — a digestive juice made by the liver to break down fats. Every time you eat fat, the gall bladder squeezes bile into the small intestine.

cards.title: How Stones Form
cards.body: Bile normally stays liquid. When bile has too much cholesterol, or when the gall bladder doesn't empty properly, bile concentrates and crystallises into stones ranging from sand-sized to golf ball-sized.

cards.title: Fatty Meals Trigger Pain
cards.body: When you eat a fatty meal, the gall bladder contracts strongly to push bile out. If a stone blocks the duct, the muscle cramps against the obstruction — causing intense pain in the upper right abdomen.

cards.title: Skipping Meals Concentrates Bile
cards.body: When you don't eat for long periods, the gall bladder doesn't squeeze and bile sits and concentrates. People who crash-diet or fast often develop gall stones. Regular small meals prevent bile from stagnating.

cards.title: Who Gets Gall Stones?
cards.body: The classic risk profile: female, forty, fat, fertile (multiple pregnancies). Oestrogen increases cholesterol in bile. Obesity, rapid weight loss, and a sedentary lifestyle are other major risk factors.

cards.title: Silent Stones
cards.body: About 80% of gall stones cause no symptoms and are found accidentally on ultrasound. Silent stones usually don't need surgery. Pain only requires intervention — don't operate on an asymptomatic stone unless recommended.

cards.title: Prevention Over Surgery
cards.body: Once removed, the gall bladder doesn't grow back — you'll need to manage fat digestion lifelong. Prevention through diet is far better than surgery. Avoid rapid weight loss, maintain healthy weight, eat regular meals.

doEat.name: Fruits (apple, pear)
doEat.note: Pectin fibre may reduce stone formation

doEat.name: Olive oil (small amounts)
doEat.note: Good fat that gently stimulates bile flow

doEat.name: Coffee (2 cups)
doEat.note: Associated with lower gall stone risk

doEat.name: Turmeric
doEat.note: Curcumin stimulates bile secretion and flow

dontEat.name: Very fatty fried foods
dontEat.note: Trigger intense gall bladder contraction and pain

dontEat.name: Refined carbs (maida, sweets)
dontEat.note: Raise blood cholesterol, more in bile

dontEat.name: Rapid crash dieting
dontEat.note: Slows gall bladder emptying, bile concentrates

dontEat.name: Skipping meals or long fasts
dontEat.note: Bile stagnates and concentrates when you don't eat

lifestyle: Eat 3 regular meals daily — never skip meals as it concentrates bile in the gall bladder.
Lose weight slowly if needed — rapid weight loss dramatically increases gall stone formation.
Exercise regularly — physical activity reduces cholesterol in bile.
Avoid very high-fat meals in a single sitting — small portions of fat are better than one large dose.
Stay well hydrated — water dilutes bile.
If you've had stones but no pain, monitor with annual ultrasound. Surgery only if symptomatic.
After gall bladder removal: eat smaller, more frequent meals and reduce fat gradually — your bile now drips constantly instead of being released in a controlled burst.
redFlags: Severe pain in the upper right abdomen lasting more than 30 minutes after a meal
Pain radiating to the right shoulder or back
Fever with upper abdominal pain — possible cholecystitis (gall bladder infection)
Yellowing of skin or eyes (jaundice) — stone blocking the bile duct
Nausea and vomiting that doesn't resolve
Clay-coloured stool and dark urine — bile duct obstruction

quiz.q: What does the gall bladder store?
quiz.why: The gall bladder stores bile made by the liver. Bile is released when you eat fat to help break it down for absorption.

quiz.q: Skipping meals can contribute to gall stone formation.
quiz.why: When you skip meals, the gall bladder doesn't empty. Bile sits and concentrates, leading to stone formation over time.

quiz.q: Why does eating a fatty meal cause pain in gall stone patients?
options: The gall bladder contracts to release bile and a stone blocks the duct
Fatty food is hard to digest
quiz.why: Fatty food triggers strong gall bladder contractions. If a stone blocks the duct, the contraction against the obstruction causes intense pain.

quiz.q: All gall stones require surgery to remove.
quiz.why: About 80% of gall stones are 'silent' (cause no symptoms). Only symptomatic stones causing pain or complications need surgery.

quiz.q: Which of these habits increases gall stone risk?
options: Crash dieting and rapid weight loss
quiz.why: Rapid weight loss slows gall bladder emptying and dramatically increases cholesterol in bile — a major trigger for stone formation.
